464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: no documented first-touch-to-first-class workflow (induction, PAR-Q, goal-setting, buddy assignment, app setup)
- **Churn prevention**: no SOP for pause/cancellation/win-back; can't track why members leave or systematically re-engage them
- **Coaching standards & QA**: no Class Quality Audit or Weekly Coach Tasks doc; no way to enforce consistency or spot underperformance early
- **Member check-ins & outcomes tracking**: no cadence for progress conversations; 30-day goals and Milestone Achievements undefined
- **Referral programme**: no process to capture, incentivize, or fulfill referrals
- **Data & compliance**: GDPR/privacy SOP missing; high legal/reputational risk
- **Supply chain**: no Order Supplies & Sundries process; ad-hoc restocking likely causing stock-outs or waste
- **Incident/complaints handling**: no Troubleshooting Guide; members with issues have no clear escalation path

**Needs updating**

- **Coaches section (20/24)**: Weekly Key Coach Tasks and Coaching Development Roadmap are gaps; existing SOPs likely missing ongoing training, performance feedback, and progression criteria
- **Clients section (23/33)**: heavy coverage but missing the *retention arc*—no documented loop between onboarding → check-in → outcomes → referral → pause/cancel
- **Marketing (5/5)**: assume these cover campaigns, but no SOP linking referral programme or win-back offers to marketing workflow
- **Facilities (5/6)**: one gap; likely missing or vague on supply ordering and stock management

**Build next**

1. **Member Onboarding SOP** (priority 1): Day-1 induction checklist →

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **AR/M collapsed to £120.48 in June (vs £165.56 in May, -27%)**. With 147 active clients, you're leaving £6,700/month on the table vs May's per-member yield. Audit pricing: are new members from the bigger studio on lower-tier plans? Implement tiered upsells (PT sessions, nutrition coaching, peak-time premium access) targeting the 25 new members added since April. Minimum target: £155 AR/M by August.

• **June revenue fell £2,488 (-12%) vs May despite adding 25 members (+20%).** The bigger studio should drive *more* revenue per head, not less. Churn was zero (excellent), but pricing/product mix is broken. Run a cohort analysis: compare Q2 joiners' spend vs Q1. If April–May members signed at higher rates, replicate that offer; if June cohort is cheaper, reset your standard pricing up by 10–15% immediately.

• **Zero churn in June is your only bright spot.** Protect it: institute a monthly retention playbook (outreach to members at risk, milestone celebrations at 3/6/12 months). One lost member at £150 AR/M = £1,800 annual revenue gone.

---

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people expenses spiked to £9,390 in June (vs £9,233 in May, minimal change, but April's £30.5k was relocation).** Benchmark rent/utilities/insurance in the new studio: are post-move costs stabilising? If non-people spend is running £9–10k/month baseline now, that's a 60% jump from pre-April (£4–5.5k). Document what's driving it (facility size, new contracts, or one-off overages). By July, lock suppliers into fixed monthly rates; target £7,500 by September.

• **Payroll has crept to £3,686 (June) from £3,037 (January baseline), +21%.** With 147 members (vs 94 in January), that's a better ratio, but June should show economies of scale from the bigger studio. Pressure test: are you overstaffed for 147 members, or do you need this team for 180+ members planned by Q4? If headcount is fixed, revenue *must* hit £24k+/month to justify £3.7k payroll. If not, restructure to part-time/freelance instructors tied to class attendance.

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's Build: Lower classes urgently** — she ran 17 sessions last month. Assign most to JoJo (who already coaches Build: Upper) to build depth in strength programming, with 1–2 to Eilis as she develops. This alone frees 17 hours from Grace.

• **Move Grace's Elevate and Strongevity to Eilis** — Eilis has capacity and already runs Elevate (7 sessions). Taking over Grace's 6 Elevate and 3 Strongevity sessions develops Eilis's class range and removes 9 more hours from Grace without stretching JoJo further.

• **Shift Grace's No Sweat Intro and Body Composition Scans to either coach** — The 3 Intro sessions and 9 Body Comp scans (28 hours combined) are low-complexity. Move scans to whoever has gaps; assign Intro to Eilis to build her member-facing confidence. Grace then has zero delivery hours.

• **Reduce JoJo's PT load by 8–10 sessions** — She ran 44 PT slots last month on top of heavy class load (133 total sessions). Move 10–12 PT clients to Eilis or consider a waiting list; JoJo is at risk of burnout and should focus on Tuesday coaching and high-value strength classes.

• **Cross-train Eilis on Build: Upper and Circuits** — Currently no PT in her profile for these. Adding 2–3 Build: Upper or Circuits sessions per month diversifies class coverage away from JoJo's shoulders and gives Eilis growth; JoJo then has breathing room.

**Fill quiet studio time:**

1. Launch a "Drop-in Barbell Clinic" (30 mins) on Monday/Thursday mornings before the first class — position it as a low-pressure technical skills workshop for members who want form feedback on Build class movements, targeting the Barbelle audience (27 attendees) who may need confidence-building before joining group classes.

2. Open a "Flex Studio" slot (60 mins) on Saturday afternoons when studio demand is lowest — offer it as paid open-gym or a small-group mobility/stretching session, since Mobility class is underattended (227 in 90 days) and Saturday is your quietest day overall.

**Lift quieter classes:**

3. Move Engine class (47 attendees) to Wednesday or Friday evening and rebrand it as "Engine: 30-min power burst" to compete on your busiest days; pair it with a pre-class email to Elevate members (308 attendees) positioning it as their "explosive finisher" with a free trial offer.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**For Coaches (Internal Menu):**
- List every service tier you *actually* deliver (group classes, 1:1 PT, nutrition coaching, mobility work, etc.) with exact pricing **you charge them to deliver it**. This forces clarity on what's profitable vs. what's eating margin. Include minimum class sizes, PT rates by experience level, any package deals you offer members.
- Add decision rules: *When does a coach recommend 1:1 vs. group?* *Can they offer discounts and to whom?* Coaches need to know boundaries so they're not freelancing your pricing.

**For Members (Public Menu):**
- Strip it down to 3-4 core offerings max (unlimited group classes, monthly coaching add-on, nutrition program, maybe a bootcamp package). Too many options = decision paralysis and confused marketing messaging.
- Price anchoring matters: Show the *most expensive* option first (e.g., full-service package), then the mid-tier, then entry-level. Psychology works—people buy the middle option.
- What's included in *each* tier must be crystal clear: unlimited classes = how many weeks? Coaching = how many sessions monthly? What happens after the first month?

**Quick execution:**
- Audit last 3 months of what members actually bought. Build your menu around *what sells*, not what you think should sell.
- Test the menu with 3-5 of your best members before rolling out—they'll spot gaps in 10 minutes that'll take you days to find.

What's your current pricing range looking like?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

1. **You've cracked lead volume and cost efficiency—but sales conversion has collapsed.** July's £3 cost/lead is the best you've ever achieved (down from £6 in April), and you've pulled 90 leads from half the spend of May. But close rate has plummeted to 8% (from 20% in April and 61% in May). You're buying cheap traffic that isn't sales-ready.

2. **May was your peak profitability month despite higher CPL.** You spent £522, closed 23 sales for £1,206 profit—nearly identical revenue to July but from half the leads. This tells you lead *quality* matters more than volume. Something about May's targeting, creative, or messaging attracted genuinely interested women; July's approach is attracting tyre-kickers and curiosity clicks.

3. **The denominator problem is real: 7 sales from 90 leads means your sales/follow-up process is the bottleneck now, not ad performance.** Even if you held CPL at £3, you'd need to double close rate to match May's profit.

**Actions**

1. **Immediately audit your July lead source by placement and audience segment.** Pull the data: which placements (Stories, Reels, Feed) and which audience/lookalike cohort generated those 90 leads? Almost certainly you've scaled into lower-intent placements (cheap Reels inventory) or broad lookalike audiences. Pause bottom-quartile performers; reallocate budget to the placements/audiences that generated May's 23 sales, even if CPL rises to £5–£7.

2. **Test two creative angles designed for qualification, not just clicks.** July's ads are optimised for lead volume. Create one set showing real member transformations + testimonial from someone who was sceptical (to filter for commitment mindset), and another explicitly mentioning the "6-week intensity" or "not for everyone" positioning. You want fewer but warmer leads. Run these at 30% of July budget and track which drives better close rate.

3. **Implement a lead-quality scoring gate before the sales team touches them.** Add a second question in your lead form:

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# The Forge — Fractional CFO Brief

## REVENUE — ARREST THE DECLINE & RECOVER YIELD

• **Revenue per member has collapsed to £112 (June) from £138 (April).** This is your biggest red flag. Excluding the relocation month, you're trending downward: £130 (Feb) → £117 (Mar) → £116 (May) → £112 (Jun). Root cause: either members aren't using classes (no upsell opportunity), or you've admitted lower-paying cohorts. **Action: audit class attendance against membership tier; identify which members pay under £100/month and either upgrade them or run targeted retention offers (personal training day pass, small-group PT, merchandise bundles) to hit £130+ per member minimum by August.**

• **Churn spiked in May (12.7%) after a clean April (3.5%).** Post-relocation churn normalised to 3.3% in June—good—but you lost 17 members in May alone. **Action: contact those 17 lapsed members within 7 days of cancellation with a "we miss you" offer (month free, or 50% off 3 months); implement automated win-back SMS at day 1 of churn.** This alone could recover £1,500–2,000 revenue run-rate.

• **You're leaving money on the table in quiet class slots.** 141 active members should fill more than you're filling. **Action: run a 2-week "bring a friend free" campaign on your lowest-attended class slots (track by TeamUp); offer a £15 referral credit per new sign-up to existing members. At a 20% conversion, this could add 5–8 members (£600–960/month).**

---

## EXPENSES — FOCUS POST-RELOCATION COST CONTROL

• **Cash outflows are now volatile: March (£23,328), April (£39,015 planned), May (£16,639), June (£20,644).** Excluding April, last 3 months average £18,637/month outflow. **Action: by end of July, produce a zero-based expense audit—rent, utilities, staff payroll, supplier contracts (cleaning, equipment maintenance, software). Flag anything above budget and negotiate: bulk discounts on supplies, off-peak utility rates, or shift one PT to commission-only (zero base + 20% of PT revenue).**

• **Payroll is likely 40–45% of revenue (£7,200–7,500 in June).** With 141 members and ~3–4 staff,
